title: Quiz: Quantum Advantage in Machine Learning
description: Test your understanding of when quantum ML provides advantages and its limitations

questions.question: Which of the following is a provably quantum-advantaged learning problem?

options.text: Learning parity with noise (with quantum data access)

questions.explanation: Learning parity with noise (LPN) has provable exponential quantum advantage when quantum access to training data is available. Classical algorithms require exponentially many samples while quantum algorithms need only O(n).

questions.question: What is the main implication of 'dequantization' results?

options.text: Quantum computers are useless for ML
Some quantum speedups can be matched classically given similar data access

questions.explanation: Dequantization results (Tang, 2018+) show that some claimed quantum exponential speedups can be matched by classical algorithms when they have similar data access models (like sample-and-query access). The quantum advantage came partly from the access model, not just quantum mechanics.

questions.question: What is the qRAM assumption and why is it problematic?

options.text: It's a fast classical memory, easily available
Quantum random access memory; not yet technologically feasible

questions.explanation: qRAM (quantum random access memory) would allow loading classical data into quantum superposition efficiently. Many quantum ML speedups assume qRAM, but building a large-scale qRAM may be as hard as building a fault-tolerant quantum computer.

questions.question: For which type of data is quantum ML most likely to provide advantage?
questions.explanation: Quantum ML is most promising for quantum mechanical data (chemistry, materials, quantum systems) where data naturally has quantum structure. For classical data, loading costs often negate speedups, and classical methods may work equally well.

questions.question: Quantum ML always requires fewer training samples than classical ML.
questions.explanation: False. Sample complexity depends on model complexity (like VC dimension), which scales similarly for quantum and classical models with the same parameter count. Quantum advantage is in expressiveness and certain computational tasks, not necessarily sample efficiency.

questions.question: When benchmarking quantum vs classical ML, what is often incorrectly excluded?

options.text: Data loading time for quantum models

questions.explanation: Data loading time is often excluded from quantum ML benchmarks. Loading N classical data points into a quantum computer takes O(N) time, which can negate claimed speedups. Fair comparisons must include all preprocessing costs.

questions.question: What makes a quantum kernel potentially advantageous over classical kernels?

options.text: It can access feature spaces that are classically hard to compute

questions.explanation: Quantum kernels can potentially access feature spaces (exponential-dimensional Hilbert space) that have no efficient classical computation. If such features are relevant to the problem and classically hard to compute, advantage is possible.

questions.question: Which are valid concerns that limit near-term quantum ML advantage? (Select all that apply)

options.text: The existence of strong classical baselines

questions.explanation: All of these limit near-term quantum ML: barren plateaus make training hard, noise degrades performance, limited qubits restrict problem size, and strong classical methods (random forests, deep learning) set a high bar to beat.
